phenomenon: Big Bang Nucleosynthesis -- Primordial Element Formation
key_measurement: BBN predicts a primordial helium-4 mass fraction Yp approximately 0.247 (about 25% of all baryonic matter by mass); observed cosmic helium abundance Yp = 0.2449 +/- 0.0040 (Aver, Olive & Skillman 2015, JCAP 07, 011); deuterium abundance D/H = (2.527 +/- 0.030) x 10^-5 by number relative to hydrogen (Cooke et al. 2018, ApJ 855, 102) -- both in strong agreement with BBN predictions at baryon-to-photon ratio eta approximately 6.1 x 10^-10
principle_set: Big Bang Nucleosynthesis (BBN) occurred in the first approximately 3-20 minutes of the universe's existence, when temperatures cooled from ~10^10 K to ~10^9 K -- the window in which nuclear reactions could form light nuclei before expansion froze them out

At T > 10^10 K (first ~1 second), the universe was a hot plasma of quarks, gluons, leptons, and photons in thermal equilibrium; protons and neutrons interconverted freely via weak interactions maintaining a neutron-to-proton ratio near 1:1

As the universe cooled below ~10^10 K (~1 second post-Big Bang), weak interactions froze out and the neutron-to-proton ratio locked in at approximately 1:7; neutrons then began decaying (half-life ~10 minutes), further reducing the ratio by the time deuterium could form

BBN produced hydrogen-1 (protons, ~75% by mass), helium-4 (~25% by mass), deuterium (~0.003% by mass), helium-3 (trace), and lithium-7 (trace ~10^-9 by number); essentially no elements heavier than beryllium were produced -- the mass-8 gap prevented further synthesis

The predicted abundances from BBN theory depend only on the baryon-to-photon ratio (eta) and the number of light neutrino species; observed cosmic abundances match predictions at eta approximately 6.1 x 10^-10, confirming the Standard Model of cosmology

BBN is one of the three pillars of Big Bang cosmology (alongside the CMB and Hubble expansion); agreement between predicted and observed light element abundances spanning 9 orders of magnitude in abundance is one of the most precise tests of early-universe physics

The lithium problem: observed primordial Li-7 abundance is approximately 3x lower than BBN predictions -- this discrepancy remains unresolved and is an active area of research, potentially involving stellar depletion of Li or new physics beyond the Standard Model
framework: Standard Big Bang cosmology; Standard Model of particle physics; BBN theory (Gamow 1940s; Alpher, Bethe, Gamow 1948; refined by Wagoner, Fowler & Hoyle 1967)
institutions: NASA Goddard Space Flight Center (WMAP cosmology team)
University of California (Olive, Skillman -- helium abundance measurements)
Cambridge Institute of Astronomy (Cooke et al. -- deuterium measurements)
University of Chicago (Kolb & Turner, The Early Universe)

sources.claim: Big Bang Nucleosynthesis produced light nuclei (deuterium, He-3, He-4, Li-7) during the first ~20 minutes of the universe; predictions rely on thermodynamic arguments and nuclear reaction rates; model agrees well with observed light element abundances
sources.citation: Wikipedia: Big Bang Nucleosynthesis; Schramm & Turner 1998, Rev. Mod. Phys. 70, 303

sources.claim: Observed primordial deuterium abundance D/H = (2.527 +/- 0.030) x 10^-5, consistent with BBN prediction at the Planck-determined baryon density
Observed primordial helium-4 mass fraction Yp = 0.2449 +/- 0.0040, consistent with Standard BBN predictions
BBN theory proposed by George Gamow; landmark alpha-beta-gamma paper (Alpher, Bethe, Gamow 1948); computational BBN calculations by Wagoner, Fowler & Hoyle 1967

visualization_notes: This subject demands the most abstract but most fundamental visual treatment in the series -- the birth of matter itself. Visual arc: open on absolute darkness and extreme density -- the universe one second old. The screen fills with a seething white-yellow plasma of pure energy, indistinguishable from light. As time advances (T+1s to T+20min shown on-screen), the plasma cools: temperature scale drops from 10 billion K to 1 billion K. Key dramatic moment 1 (T approximately 1 second): weak interaction freeze-out -- the neutron-to-proton ratio locks at 1:7. Visualize as two types of glowing particles unable to interconvert. Key dramatic moment 2 (T approximately 3 minutes): deuterium bottleneck breaks -- previously photons were energetic enough to smash apart any deuterium that formed; now the universe is cool enough for deuterium to survive. The first atomic nuclei appear -- visualized as small glowing clusters. Key dramatic moment 3 (T approximately 20 minutes): BBN ends as temperature drops too low for fusion. The universe is now 75% hydrogen, 25% helium -- frozen in this ratio for approximately 380,000 years until recombination. Color palette: searing white-yellow plasma cooling to orange, then amber, then dimmer red as expansion continues. Scale insight: the entire observable universe (93 billion light-years diameter) compressed to a region smaller than the Solar System during BBN. Most cinematically powerful concept: show a gold bar on-screen -- None of this existed at T+20 minutes. All heavy elements came later. Available public images: NASA/WMAP CMB maps (publicly released); ESA/Planck CMB maps. No direct images of BBN exist -- all visualization is artistic, which is standard for this subject.
visualization_compliance: No classified data -- all BBN research is open academic literature
CMB maps (WMAP/Planck) are publicly released by NASA and ESA -- credit NASA/WMAP Science Team and ESA/Planck Collaboration required
Artistic depictions of early universe conditions are standard in documentary production and permissible
Abundance values cited must match published measurements; the lithium problem should be acknowledged as unresolved if discussed
George Gamow is a historical figure -- archival imagery may be available through public domain sources
